title: Unentangled stoquastic Merlin-Arthur proof systems: the power of unentanglement without destructive interference
abstract: Stoquasticity, originating in sign-problem-free physical systems, gives rise to $\sf StoqMA$, introduced by Bravyi, Bessen, and Terhal (2006), a quantum-inspired intermediate class between $\sf MA$ and $\sf AM$. Unentanglement similarly gives rise to ${\sf QMA}(2)$, introduced by Kobayashi, Matsumoto, and Yamakami (CJTCS 2009), which generalizes $\sf QMA$ to two unentangled proofs and still has only the trivial $\sf NEXP$ upper bound. In this work, we initiate a systematic study of the power of unentanglement without destructive interference via ${\sf StoqMA}(2)$, the class of unentangled stoquastic Merlin-Arthur proof systems. Although $\sf StoqMA$ is semi-quantum and may collapse to $\sf MA$, ${\sf StoqMA}(2)$ turns out to be surprisingly powerful. We establish the following results:   - ${\sf NP} \subseteq {\sf StoqMA}(2)$ with $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{n})$-qubit proofs and completeness error $2^{-{\rm polylog}(n)}$. Conversely, ${\sf StoqMA}(2) \subseteq {\sf EXP}$ via the Sum-of-Squares algorithm of Barak, Kelner, and Steurer (STOC 2014); with our lower bound, our refined analysis yields the optimality of this algorithm under ETH.   - ${\sf StoqMA}(2)_1 \subseteq {\sf PSPACE}$, and the containment holds with completeness error $2^{-2^{{\rm poly}(n)}}$.   - ${\sf PreciseStoqMA}(2)$, a variant of ${\sf StoqMA}(2)$ with exponentially small promise gap, cannot achieve perfect completeness unless ${\sf EXP}={\sf NEXP}$. In contrast, ${\sf PreciseStoqMA}$ achieves perfect completeness, since ${\sf PSPACE} \subseteq {\sf PreciseStoqMA}_1$.   - When the completeness error is negligible, ${\sf StoqMA}(k) = {\sf StoqMA}(2)$ for $k\geq 2$.   Our lower bounds are obtained by stoquastizing the short-proof ${\sf QMA}(2)$ protocols via distribution testing techniques. Our upper bounds for the nearly perfect completeness case are proved via our new rectangular closure testing framework.
summary: This paper explores the complexity of quantum proof systems that are both unentangled and stoquastic. It demonstrates that even without the power of destructive interference, unentangled stoquastic systems can capture significant complexity classes like NP and PSPACE.
main_problem: The paper investigates the computational power and structural properties of the complexity class StoqMA(2), which involves unentangled (separable) quantum proofs within sign-problem-free (stoquastic) systems.
main_result: The authors establish that StoqMA(2) is surprisingly powerful, containing NP and having upper bounds in EXP and PSPACE, while showing that unentanglement provides significant power even without destructive interference.
method: The study employs techniques such as stoquastization of QMA(2) protocols, distribution testing, the Sum-of-Squares (SoS) algorithm, and a new rectangular closure testing framework.
model_or_system: The model consists of stoquastic Merlin-Arthur proof systems where the witness is a separable state across two registers and the underlying Hamiltonian is sign-problem-free (non-negative off-diagonal elements).
key_observables: Completeness and soundness errors, promise gaps, and acceptance probabilities.
important_parameters: Number of provers (k), proof length (qubit count), and the presence of perfect completeness or exponentially small promise gaps.
main_assumptions: The optimality results are stated under the Exponential-Time Hypothesis (ETH).
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a summary diagram of the relationships between various complexity classes like StoqMA(2), QMA(2), NP, and PSPACE; Figure 3 illustrates the architecture of a verifier for the intersection of promise problems.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the StoqMA(2) class, establishes lower bounds via distribution testing, derives upper bounds using the SoS algorithm and rectangular closure testing, and concludes with an analysis of precise and symmetric variants.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Relationships among StoqMA(2), its variants, and other complexity classes.
Figure 3: Conjunction of stoquastic verifiers V (1) x1 , V (2) x2 , and V (3) x3 .
